But one gap remained: the ability to add entirely new entities to the Business Partner data model inside the governance and consolidation process. That gap is now closed.

What Is a Node Extension?

A node extension means adding a completely new child entity to an existing Business Partner object — without modifying any SAP standard code.
Think of the Business Partner as a tree. Existing branches are General Data, Addresses, Bank Accounts, Roles, and so on. A node extension lets you grow a new branch — your own custom entity with its own fields, UI, and governance lifecycle — and attach it cleanly to that tree.
The result: your custom data (say, "Financial Org Data" or "Sustainability Metrics") participates fully in the MDG governance process. It gets staged, reviewed, approved, and activated just like any standard Business Partner attribute.

What the How-To Guide Covers
SAP has published a comprehensive How-to Guide: Develop Node Extensions for Business Partner, which walks developers through the full implementation. Here is a brief overview of the key steps.

1. Define the Data Model
Start by extending the data model used by MDG’s cloud-ready framework with your custom table. The framework then automatically generates the necessary database artifacts ready to be connected to the governance process.

2. Build the CDS View Layer
Create a set of CDS views that expose your custom data within the Business Partner object hierarchy — for both the consolidation process and the governance context.

3. Extend the Business Partner Objects
Using standard CDS and behavior extension mechanisms, wire your new node into the existing Business Partner objects — for both the process and governance scenarios. No modification of SAP standard objects is required.

4. Define and Implement the Transactional Behavior
Specify how your node behaves at runtime: which operations are allowed, when fields are editable, how bulk Mass Edit is supported, and how changes are persisted to the staging area. The guide provides complete, ready-to-use code samples for all required methods.

5. Register MDG Application Classes
Plug your extension into MDG's responsibility framework by creating and registering a small set of application-specific classes. These handle dynamic field mapping, governance persistence, feature control, and modification status tracking — all configurable via standard MDG IMG settings.

6. Expose to the UI
Extend the OData service definition to include your new entity, and add Fiori Elements annotations to configure how your data appears as a new tab in the Business Partner UI — with proper labels, sort order, filters, and field visibility.

The Result

Once complete, the custom node is fully integrated into the MDG Cloud-Ready Business Partner process:

  • A new tab appears in the process UI alongside General, Roles, Addresses, and other standard sections — with per-record change indicators and Mass Edit support
  • The governance UI shows custom data fully integrated with the Business Partner data
  • Custom records are staged, approved, and activated together with the rest of the Business Partner data
  • The solution is upgrade-safe — no SAP standard objects are modified, fully aligned with clean core principles

Who Should Read This Guide?

  • SAP implementation partners building MDG Cloud-Ready solutions for customers with non-standard Business Partner data needs
  • Customer development teams looking to migrate existing custom BP extensions to Cloud-Ready Mode
  • Enterprise architects evaluating MDG Cloud-Ready extensibility before committing to the platform
  • Developers who want a concrete, end-to-end example of node extensibility in an MDG context

Looking Ahead

You can now manage custom Business Partner data using the same controls, workflows, and modern interface as standard SAP data. It's upgrade-safe and cloud-ready.

We encourage you to explore the How-to-Guide and share your feedback with the MDG product team.
The future of Business Partner governance is extensible — and it's here now.

At this point, a big thank you to:
Santhosh Kumar Cheekoti / Development Principal Consultant, who wrote the How-To Guide with great expertise!
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Tags: SAP Master Data Governance | MDG Cloud-Ready Mode | Business Partner | Node Extensibility | SAP S/4HANA 2025 | Clean Core | SAP Community

Have questions or feedback? Join the conversation in the SAP Community — we'd love to hear how you're using node extensions in your MDG implementations.

entry.title: Working with BDC in the SAP Business AI Platform: The Data Analyst Perspective
entry.content:
As a data analyst working with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), you already rely on governed data models, consistent KPIs, and trusted semantics to produce analytics and insights. The Autonomous Enterprise strategy with the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) doesn’t change that foundation, it extends it.

What does change is how insights are generated, explained, and acted on. Analytics is no longer the final step in the data value chain. It becomes an active input into AI‑driven reasoning and execution, grounded in the same business context that you define and validate today. At the core of this shift is how BDC participates in the Contextualize and Reason services of BAIP, alongside the SAP Knowledge Graph. BDC provides the harmonized data foundation and business semantics, while the SAP Knowledge Graph encodes relationships and business context. Together, they form the trusted context layer that AI agents rely on to reason and act.

What stays the same

Your core analytical responsibilities remain intact. You continue to work with semantic models defined in SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, and SAP HANA Cloud, accessing SAP and non-SAP data, where business meaning is explicitly modeled. These models, together with master data governed through SAP Master Data Governance, form the structured input to the knowledge graph that AI relies on. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) remains your primary environment for analysis, visualization, and planning.

The difference is that these analytical assets are no longer only consumed by humans, they are part of the contextual foundation used by AI agents.

What changes in practice

The most important shift is that AI now consumes the same semantic and contextual layer you work with. In the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents do not generate insights from isolated datasets. They reason over BDC data products enriched with relationships captured in the SAP Knowledge Graph. When an AI agent produces a narrative, explains a deviation, or recommends an action, it operates on top of business objects, hierarchies, and dependencies that you already know.

This changes the validation process. Instead of questioning the origin of an AI insight, you can trace it back to governed data products and their relationships within the broader business context. Analytics shifts from being a downstream consumer of data to being a shared semantic contract between humans and AI.

How AI‑assisted analytics works with BDC

AI‑assisted insight generation doesn’t bypass your analytical models. It builds on them. Data products defined in BDC, whether sourced from SAP applications, BW objects, or Datasphere models, carry business meaning, lineage, and access rules. BAIP uses these data products as the context layer for AI reasoning. When AI generates narratives, summaries, or explanations, it does so against this governed semantic layer.

For you as a data analyst, this means faster exploration and explanation without sacrificing consistency. You spend less time reconciling definitions and more time interpreting results.

Impact on trust and validation

One of the biggest challenges with AI‑generated insights is trust. With BAIP, trust is achieved by design. Because AI agents rely on the same BDC‑managed semantics you use, AI outputs inherit the same KPI logic, hierarchies, and filters. Access controls and data policies are enforced consistently, whether the consumer is a dashboard, a planner, or an AI agent.

This dramatically reduces the need for parallel validation loops and manual reconciliation.

How this maps to BDC components

For data analysts, the component mapping is straightforward.

  • SAP Datasphere continues to be the primary place where analytical semantics are modeled and exposed as data products.
  • SAP BW continues to provide enterprise‑grade, historically rich business logic that remains authoritative for many KPIs.
  • SAP HANA Cloud supports advanced analytical queries and high‑performance access to data products.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the main consumption layer for analysis, planning, and increasingly AI‑assisted insight generation.
  • SAP Master Data Governance ensures that master data consistency underpins all analytical and AI‑driven outcomes.

Together, these components form the trusted analytical foundation that AI agents consume through BAIP.

What to focus on now

As a data analyst, the most valuable preparation is not learning new AI tools. It is strengthening the analytical foundation AI will rely on. Focus on well‑defined KPIs, stable semantics, clear lineage, and reusable data products. The better your analytical models are today, the more reliable AI‑assisted insights will be tomorrow.

The takeaway

BAIP doesn’t replace analytics with AI. It connects analytics and AI through a shared semantic foundation. For data analysts, this means higher leverage: the models you build and validate do not just inform people, they inform AI‑driven decisions as well.

Join the Data Professionals community discussions to share analytical patterns, discuss AI‑assisted insights, and explore how others are evolving their BDC analytics foundations in the Autonomous Enterprise era.

This post is a follow up to the previous post From ‘AI in Applications’ to ‘AI on Applications’. It is the first in a series that explores how the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) impacts day‑to‑day work across key BDC personas, starting with the data analyst perspective.

entry.title: Your AI Agent Has Three Different Definitions of "Customer." That's a Problem.
entry.content:
Source: IDEA Forum, NYC 2025

My last post made one argument: most AI projects don't stall because of the model. They stall because of the data underneath it. This post is about the most common culprit - the one that tends to surprise people, because they thought they'd already solved it.

---

## Three records. One customer. Zero clarity.

Here's the scenario.

You deploy an internal AI copilot connected to your CRM, billing system, and logistics platform. A sales manager asks it to pull together everything the company knows about a key account before a renewal meeting. The agent returns an answer - confident, formatted, comprehensive.

And it's wrong. Not because the AI failed. Because the customer has three different records across three different systems, each updated independently, none of them authoritative.

This isn't a data quality story. It's a structural one. Most large organizations carry fragmented or duplicated records for every core business entity - customers, vendors, products, employees. Humans have always navigated that fragmentation with judgment: they know which system to trust for which question. That institutional knowledge is invisible, undocumented, and entirely absent from the AI.

The agent doesn't apply judgment. It uses whatever it can access, and it uses it with confidence.

---

## What the ODI framework calls this

The ODI's Framework for AI-Ready Enterprise Data treats this as a baseline condition of enterprise data, not an edge case. It names Master Data Management, establishing a single authoritative "golden record" for each core entity, as one of six enterprise-specific criteria that separate organizations ready to scale AI from those that aren't.

The framing matters. MDM isn't new. Most SAP shops have some version of it in place. What the framework adds is a recast: MDM is no longer a data governance discipline. In an agentic world, it's AI infrastructure.

Without it, every AI system querying across your data is operating on an unverified, inconsistent foundation. The errors that produces are systematically difficult to trace. You won't see them at first. You'll see them six months in, when someone starts pulling on a thread and finds the model was confident about the wrong version of reality.

---

## What MDM at scale actually requires

The framework is specific about what this takes operationally. Designating records as authoritative isn't enough. You need entity resolution pipelines — continuous processes that actively scan across disparate systems, identify duplicates, and merge them into validated, governed golden records. Not a one-time clean-up. An ongoing operational capability.

That distinction matters. Organizations that treat MDM as a project to complete are the same ones rebuilding it two years into an AI initiative, under pressure, with a more complicated data landscape than they started with.

---

## Where SAP Business Data Cloud sits in this

This is the design premise of SAP Business Data Cloud: harmonized, business-ready data objects that unify SAP application data, S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, Ariba, and the broader SAP ecosystem, into a governed, consistent layer that AI systems can trust.

The logic is direct. AI performs reliably only on data it can verify. You can't verify data without a single authoritative source. Business Data Cloud is the mechanism for establishing that source - not as a static warehouse, but as a live, governed business data layer that persists as AI workloads run against it.

For anyone evaluating or implementing Business Data Cloud: the ODI framework gives that investment independent validation. Harmonizing business objects, resolving duplicates, establishing governed master records, that's not data hygiene. It's the prerequisite for every AI use case you're planning to build on top of it.

---

## The compounding problem

There's a specific risk the framework names worth sitting with. AI systems don't just consume data, they generate it. Classifications, summaries, derived records, confidence scores. As deployments scale, AI-generated data accumulates alongside source data. Without MDM, that generated data starts to obscure original truth, and the further you get from that moment, the harder it becomes to identify which record is authoritative.

The framework calls this the "single source of truth" problem compounding under AI load. The organizations that address it before scaling will have a significantly easier time than those that retrofit it afterward.

How many authoritative versions of "customer" does your organization actually have right now? Most people I ask pause before answering.

---

## Two ways to go deeper

The ODI framework publishes at the end of June. IDEA membership is free - it includes early access to the full report and a peer community of senior data leaders working through exactly these questions.

[Join IDEA]

On June 25th, the ODI and SAP are hosting a live webinar, Building the Foundation: AI-Ready Data for the Modern Enterprise,  working through the framework directly with the team that built it.

[Register for the June 25th webinar]

---

IDEA (the Interchange for Data and Enterprise AI) is a programme of independent research and peer learning developed in partnership between SAP and the Open Data Institute. Nine roundtables. 140+ senior executives. Three research briefs. And a framework that's about to change how enterprise AI readiness gets measured.

entry.title: SAP MDG AI - Joule Capabilities Overview & Configuration
entry.content:
Overview:

SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) is increasingly enhanced with generative AI and Joule (SAP’s AI copilot) to simplify master data creation, maintenance, and governance. These AI capabilities reduce manual effort, improve data quality, and allow business users to perform complex MDG tasks using natural language without deep technical knowledge.

Key benefits include:

  • Natural language processing (NLP) for creating and updating master data change requests.
  • AI-generated summaries of changes for faster review and approval.
  • Intelligent assistance for Business Partners, Materials, Finance, and custom objects.
  • Faster governance processes with proactive insights and automation.

These features are primarily available in SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS0 and higher (especially Cloud Private Edition / Central Governance).

Main Discovery Center Entry:

AI Feature - Joule Capabilities for Master Data Governance

Capabilities:

  • Joule Capabilities for Master Data Governance. Use conversational AI to create, update, and review master data via natural language prompts.
  • AI-Assisted Change Requests: Generate change requests from plain English descriptions.
  • AI-Generated Summaries: Automatically summarize changes in a change request for quicker governance and approval.
  • Support for Multiple Domains: Strong support for Business Partner (BP), with expanding support for materials, finance, and custom/flex entities.
  • Official Help Link: Joule Capabilities for Master Data Governance
  • This video describes new Joule Capabilities in MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, such as creating and editing Business Partner data.
  • 3634627 - Joule Capabilities for Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Cloud

Components:

  • CA-MDG-CMP-JOULE (Joule Content for Consolidation and Mass Processing in MDG)

SAP Notes (Important references):

  • 3634627 - Joule Capabilities for Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Cloud
  • 3562330 - MDG: Implementation guide for AI-assisted change for custom entities - SAP for Me
  • 3573659 - AI-assisted change request in Master Data Governance, Central Governance for S/4HANA Cloud
  • 3526419 - MDG-AF: Supported entities and attributes for AI-assisted change - SAP for Me

Configuration and Prerequisites:

  • Minimum Required Version: SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS0 (Feature Pack Stack 0) or higher.
  • Activation of relevant Joule scenarios and AI services (via SAP BTP / Generative AI Hub where applicable).
  • Proper user authorizations for MDG apps and Joule.
  • For custom entities: Follow implementation guides in the relevant SAP Notes.
  • Review Master data governance Joule capabilities for a list of prerequisites.  

AI-Specific Features and resources in SAP MDG:

  • AI-Assisted Features: New AI-Assisted Feature in SAP Master Data Governance
  • Discovery Center AI Feature Page: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, master data governance
  • Community Blogs (great for overviews and examples):
    • SAP Document AI in MDG via ISLM
    • Search SAP Community for "MDG AI" or "Joule MDG" for more recent posts

Product roadmap:

  • View the latest planned innovations in the Roadmap for MDG and AI: Road map Explorer
  • What's New in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, master data governance​ - Release 2025 Pages 19-24 include Joule AI co-pilot for SAP Master data governance.

Additional Useful Links:

  • Joule Setup Guide: Comprehensive Setup Blog
  • MDG Standard Authorization Objects & Roles: Authorization Objects Used by MDG, Central Governance

entry.title: Bring your own Data Provider to SAP Master Data Governance for Business Partner
entry.content:
Your business partner data is only as good as the sources behind it. And here is the great news: SAP Master Data Governance now lets you connect any external reference data provider you trust - whether that's a commercial registry, a regional authority database, or a specialized industry source.

No core modifications. No custom SAP MDG code. All provider specific integration code is outsourced to SAP Cloud Integration.

How It Works

SAP Master Data Governance provides a provider-agnostic interface. In SAP Cloud Integration you copy and adapt a ready-made integration template for your provider - request format, authentication, response mapping - and deploy it. Your users search for business partners, pick the best match, and key data like tax numbers and identification numbers flow directly into the creation process.

Why It's Worth It

  • Use the provider that fits your business - not just the one that comes pre-configured by SAP
  • Run multiple providers in parallel - users choose at runtime, no IT involvement needed
  • Stay upgrade-safe - your customizations are isolated from SAP's core framework
  • Get up and running fast - copy SAP's delivered template and adapt only what's provider-specific

Interested? Explore the How-To-Guide  to get up to speed with your integration.

For questions about SAP Master Data Governance and data provider integration, please use our our  discussion forum in the SAP Community, and feel free to give us your feedback by commenting on this blog post.

To stay up to date with what’s going on in master data management and governance, simply follow the SAP Community Topic Page for SAP Master Data Governance.

entry.title: How SAP MDG lets you download and fix problem records directly from your process
entry.content:
Introduction

If you have ever run a mass change or consolidation process in SAP Master Data Governance, you know how valuable the validation step is. However, when the validation step stops due to errors, the erroneous records are stuck inside the system. Error messages are visible, but there was no way to extract, fix, and re-upload them.

And when a file upload fails due to invalid source IDs, all you get was a generic message like "Load contains invalid source IDs: 2". No details, no way to tell which records are actually affected. Either way, users end up rebuilding files manually.

This pain point was also one of the most strongly requested topics in SAP Customer Influence, with many customers highlighting the need for a smoother way to handle failed records. This was addressed, both following the same principle: giving a possibility to download a file of the problematic records, in exactly the format needed to fix and re-upload.

Feature 1: Export Process Data with Messages in the Validation Step

The Problem

In a consolidation or mass change process, the validation step checks the record correctness. When errors are found, the process stops and shows aggregated KPIs (e.g., "47 records with errors"). You can drill into the Details UI to see which records failed and why, but there was no way to export that subset for offline correction and re-upload.

What Was Delivered

The details UI of the validation step directly includes a new export action. When pressing the button it navigates to the export app and the filter for selected records is automatically set.

What it does:

  • Exports all records currently displayed in the validation step's overview tab
  • Includes the related messages (errors, warnings) as an additional sheet/section in the exported file
  • Uses the standard MDG export file format, the exported file can be edited offline and re-uploaded directly into a new import or mass processing run

After downloading the file errors and warnings are shown in the ! Messages ! tab of your excel sheet:

Availability

  • MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2022
  • SAP S/4HANA cloud public edition

Feature 2: Download Invalid Records During Upload

The Problem

When uploading a file for a mass process, the system performs a technical validation, e.g. it checks whether the IDs in the file exist in the system. If they do not, it raises a generic error:

"Load contains invalid source IDs: 3"

That is all the users got, just generic information, no more details. Which three records? Which IDs? Users had to manually inspect the file, compare it with the system, and figure it out themselves.

What Was Delivered

Whenever invalid records in a file are detected, it is possible now to export those records from the original file by an “Export”-button in File Import Log area.

The file is in the same format as the upload, so users can correct the records (fix or remove the invalid IDs) and re-upload without rebuilding the file from scratch.

Availability

  • MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2023 in classic mode
  • MDG on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 in cloud-ready mode
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Why These Two Features Matter Together

Both features address the same need: when something goes wrong in an MDG process, users need a way to act quickly. With both features, you can download the problematic records as a file, fix them offline, and use that file as the starting point for a follow-up process.

Feature 1 helps when errors are found during the validation step. Feature 2 helps even earlier, when the file upload itself fails due to invalid IDs. In both cases, the result is the same: a downloadable file with exactly the records that need to be fixed. This means users no longer must rebuild files from scratch or investigate manually which records caused the error. This saves time, reduces the load on technical teams, and makes the whole process more reliable, especially when working with large data volumes where even a small error rate can mean hundreds of records to fix.

Have you ever used one of these features?

_________________

Further Links and Resources

  • SAP Master Data Governance community https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/master-data-governance
  • SAP Help Portal https://help.sap.com/docs/MASTER_DATA_GOVERNANCE - Various help documents for e.g. MDG on S/4HANA Cloud Private and Cloud Public Edition
  • SAP Influence Portal https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#campaign/3497 - SAP Master Data Governance on SAP S/4HANA: Submit new ideas or vote on existing requests to shape the MDG roadmap
  • SAP Learning https://learning.sap.com/courses/sap-master-data-governance-on-sap-s-4hana - Learning content covering MDG strategy, domains and processes related to MDG central governance, consolidation and mass processing end-to-end

entry.title: Data Management in the Age of AI: Lessons from the Field
entry.content:
Reflections from a conversation with Chris Sam, Principal Architect at SAP and a seasoned SAP data management practitioner and consultant

Every so often, you have a conversation that crystallizes decades of industry wisdom into a few clear themes. That's what happened during a recent interview with Chris Sam, a data professional who has spent 18 years at SAP working across the full spectrum of data management. From building data warehouses in the early days, to SAP Business Objects tools like SAP Data Services and SAP Information Steward, to today's end-to-end data governance and conversion work in ERP implementations, his journey mirrors the evolution of our discipline itself.

What emerged from our conversation wasn't a list of shiny new techniques or product pitches. Instead, it was something more valuable: a grounded, honest set of best practices for organizations that want to treat data as the strategic asset it truly is. Here's what he shared, and why it matters now more than ever.

From Tactical Firefighting to Holistic Strategy

One of the first observations he made cut straight to the heart of the problem: "A lot of customers are ready to do the tactical activity — governance, cleansing, profiling — but I don't see a holistic strategy."

This is a pattern many of us in the data community recognize. Organizations jump into projects because something is broken. A migration is looming. A dashboard shows conflicting numbers. An AI initiative stalls because the data isn't trustworthy. But without a coherent end-to-end vision — one that treats data as an asset rather than a commodity — those tactical wins rarely add up to lasting transformation.

He put it simply: "Every company is a data company now. Data is foundational for everything." And with AI now dominating conversations at every customer site, that foundational role has become impossible to ignore. AI requires high-quality data. Full stop. Without a data management strategy in place, AI ambitions collapse under the weight of inconsistent, incomplete, or ungoverned information.

Data Management is a Journey, not a Switch

When asked for his top piece of advice, he offered a deceptively simple principle: data management is a journey.

"It's not like you just flip on a button and it automatically fixes everything," he said. "It takes time. It takes investment. And it takes understanding why it's important."

He acknowledged, with a laugh, that data isn't glamorous. It's not a flashy dashboard or a beautiful visualization. Most people in an organization don't understand it, which is why it gets overlooked and underestimated. But those of us who love the discipline know: without clean, well-governed data underneath, none of the pretty stuff works.

Organizational Commitment Is Non-Negotiable

The second-best practice he emphasized — and returned to throughout the conversation — was organizational commitment. This is where he sees the biggest gap in the customers he serves.

"When I go into customers and talk to the business folks and different stewards, without proper ownership, they don't take me seriously. Data has to be important to that organization."

He recounted an experience from his years at a major energy company, where the analytics platform was already delivering tremendous value, but the absence of clear data management principles meant the organization was leaving significant value on the table. Timestamps weren't being used consistently. Everyone was extracting their own customer data. If a senior leader asked five people how many customers the company had, they got five wildly different answers — not even in the same ballpark.

The fix wasn't a new tool. It was consistency, definitions, and ownership. A data dictionary, a shared understanding of what "customer" or "account" even means. And most importantly, someone accountable of the definition and data standards.

Who Needs to Be at the Table?

This led naturally to a question that plagues every data management initiative: who needs to be involved? Chris’s answer was clear: data owners and stewards — real ones, not nominal ones — and he was candid about how hard they are to find.

"Many people I encounter on the customer side are operational. They're trying to get a task done — ship materials, send invoices. They don't have that clear data owner saying, 'Making sure material data is high quality, it's correct, it's extended to the right plants — that's my responsibility.'"

The people who typically show up to data meetings, he noted, are either core business team members who understand processes but not the underlying data, or IT team members who understand the technical layer but not the business meaning. The magic happens with data stewards, people who sit in the line of business, know how the process works, know the data, and are passionate about both.

That combination is rare. And it's usually not someone's full-time job. But that role, he insisted, is critical infrastructure for any organization serious about data.

Data Products and Consistency at Scale

One of the most encouraging shifts he's seeing is the rise of data products, the idea of productizing customer, vendor, and material data so that everyone in the organization is looking at the same trusted source.

"They didn't want random people dumping tables and coming up with their own version of the truth," he said, describing what customers were trying to solve nearly a decade ago. Today, with platforms like SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and SAP Master Data Governance (MDG), that vision is more achievable than ever.

He also highlighted an often-overlooked truth: data quality must be enforced at the point of creation. Whether through user entry validation or API-level checks during integration, catching bad data at the source is far more effective than cleaning it up downstream after it has spread across systems.

Closing Thoughts

Perhaps the most striking part of our conversation was the sense of vindication — and mild exasperation — that comes from watching an industry finally catch up to what data professionals have been saying for 20 years: Data quality matters. Governance matters. Ownership matters. AI hasn't changed that; it has only made it urgent.

The path forward isn't complicated, but it isn't easy either. Start with strategy. Commit organizationally. Find your data owners. Define your terms. Treat data as a product. And remember, it's a journey.

The rest of us data geeks will keep cheering you on.

 

Interested in learning more about Data Governance?  Check out:

  • From Silos to Strategy: What 20 Years of SAP Data Management Taught One Practitioner
  • The Challenges You Face and How SAP Business Data Cloud Helps (focused on Data Stewards and the topic of Data Governance)
  • The Day in the Life of a Data Steward – Meet Susan
  • Why master data management is the foundation of AI and digital transformation

entry.title: SAP MDG Cloud Ready Mode: UI Modeling with local annotation files for App F7204A (Manage BP)
entry.content:
How To…

Hide the «Create-Button for Bank Details" in the MDG App “Manage Business Partner – Central Governance  (F7204A)

Date: July 2026, Author: Steffen Ulmer

Goal:

When working with the standard SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) Fiori app F7204A, the Bank Details section is displayed — including a Create button — whenever a user holds the authorization to edit or create a Business Partner record. While this is the expected behavior for standard data maintenance, it can become a compliance concern in governance-driven scenarios.

In MDG governance processes, it is sometimes necessary to restrict end users from creating new (and deleting existing) bank details directly, even if they are otherwise authorized to work with Business Partner records. This guide walks you through a practical, technical approach to hiding the "Create" button in the Bank Details section of app F7204A using local annotation files.

Info

  • Scope note: This guide uses a local annotation file approach, which gives you fine-grained control over UI element visibility and field properties in MDG Fiori apps.
  • Key User Extensibility ("Adapt UI") is not applicable for hiding these buttons in app F7204A. Although Adapt UI is typically the simplest option for UI adjustments, the architectural design of this app prevents button-level hiding through that feature.
  • The technique described here is reusable: the same pattern applies to other action buttons and can also be used to influence data field behavior within the same section.

Before / After Screenshots

Before:

After:

Prerequisites: SAP BTP BAS – UI Adaptation Project & Deployment

SAP MDG on SAP S/4HANA

  • SAP S/4HANA Version PCE (Private Cloud Edition) >=2025

BAS Access

  • Enterprise global account in SAP BTP
  • Subaccount with BAS entitlement configured and subscription created via Service Marketplace
  • Business_Application_Studio_Developer role collection assigned to your user
  • Supported browser: Mozilla Firefox, Google Chrome, or Microsoft Edge

Space & Project Setup

  • Destination to the ABAP system configured in SAP BTP (with HTML5.DynamicDestination and HTML5.Timeout properties)
  • If on-premise: SAP Cloud Connector installed, configured, and connected to the subaccount

Deploy App Variant

  1. User assigned role SAP_UI_FLEX_DEVELOPER (SAP_UI ≥ 7.55) or S_DEVELOP (SAP_UI < 7.55)
  2. Active transport request available in the ABAP system
  3. After deployment: configure a new target mapping in SAP Fiori Launchpad Designer for the new app variant tile

Step by Step

Step 1: Set Up BAS Space

  1. Log on to SAP BTP Business Application Studio.
  2. Use an existing SAP Fiori-type dev space, or create a new one of type SAP Fiori

Step 2: Create the Adaptation Project

  • From the BAS home screen, choose New Project from Template

  • Select SAPUI5 Adaptation Project and click Start

  • Select ABAP as the target environment and click Next.
  • Choose the destination pointing to your SAP S/4HANA system


Note: The system will connect to the backend and read all apps available for adaptation — this may take a moment.

  • From the application list, select: F7204A – Manage Business Partner – Central Governance

  • Complete the wizard by entering:
    • Project Name
    • Application Title
    • Namespace
  • Click Finish to generate the project.

Step 3: Implement the Local Annotation

  • Open the Adaptation Editor: In the Project Explorer, right-click the file manifest.appdescr_variant.
  • Select Open Adaptation Editor.

  • The app opens in the visual editor. Ensure you are on the UI Adaptation tab.
  • Navigate to the Bank Details Section: Switch to Navigation mode within the editor. Search for a Business Partner and navigate into the BP record. Click Edit — the Bank Details section and its Create button will become visible.
  • Add a Local Annotation File: From the Quick Actions panel, choose Add Local Annotation File

  • Select Main Service as the target OData service

  • Save the changes.


The system generates a new annotation XML file under webapp/changes/annotations/ in your project folder.

Step 4: Edit the Annotation XML

  • Switch back to the BAS browser tab and open the generated XML file.

 

  • Insert the following lines at line 16 of the XML file:
<Annotations Target="SAP__self.Container/BusinessPartnerBank">    
<Annotation Term="UI.CreateHidden" Bool="true"/>    
<Annotation Term="UI.DeleteHidden" Bool="true"/>
</Annotations> 

 

These annotations hide the Create and Delete buttons in the Bank Details section of the Business Partner Object Page.

  • Preview the Variant - Open the variant in Preview mode.

 

Verify that the Create and Delete buttons are no longer visible in the Bank Details section.

 

Step 5: Post-Deployment Steps

The following steps are required to make the variant available to end users but are not specific to this use case.

Step

Action

Deploy

Deploy the adaptation project to the target SAP S/4HANA system. Ensure the correct Transport Request and ABAP package are specified.

Catalog Management

After deployment, include the Fiori App Variant in the relevant Fiori catalogs used by the target business roles.

 

Further Reference: For detailed guidance on deploying adaptation projects and managing catalogs, refer to the SAP Fiori Tools – Deploying an Adaptation Project documentation.

 

 

Appendix: How to change field properties

If you want to change the field property you can also add the details to the created annotation file.  This example changes the filed "Bank Account" to read only:

 

<Annotations Target="SAP__self.BusinessPartnerBankType/BankAccount">

<Annotation Term="Common. FieldControl">

<EnumMember >Common.FieldControlType/ReadOn1y</EnumMember>

</Annotation>

</Annotations>

 

These are the steps you might need to do to find the Target for your relevant field:

  1. Identify the target for the field: Open ODATA service in chrome https://<server>:<port>/sap/opu/odata4/sap/c_businesspartnergovtp_v4/srvd/sap/c_businesspartnergovtp_srv/0001/$metadata?sap-language=EN
  2. In the metadata file search for the relevant field
  4. Copy/Remember the target path
  5. go to the annotation file in BAS and the code snippet

  6.

entry.title: Working with BDC in the Autonomous Enterprise: The Enterprise Architect Perspective
entry.content:
AI will not replace enterprise architecture. It will depend on it.

For enterprise architects working with SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC), that single idea reframes the Autonomous Enterprise. Your mandate hasn’t changed. You still ensure the structural integrity of the enterprise data and application landscape through governed architectures, trusted semantics, and scalable integration. What has changed is who, and what, your architecture now serves.

With the SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP), AI agents join humans and applications as first-class consumers of the enterprise landscape. Your architecture becomes the semantic contract that binds all three to a shared source of truth.

 

What stays the same

Your core responsibilities are intact:

  • Designing end-to-end data flows across SAP Datasphere, SAP BW, SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP HANA Cloud, SAP Master Data Governance, and SAP Reltio.
  • Defining integration patterns across SAP and non-SAP systems.
  • Establishing standards for identity, security, lineage, lifecycle, scalability, resilience, cost, and compliance.

None of this is sidelined. In fact, it matters more, because AI now depends on it.

 

What changes in practice

The biggest shift: AI agents are now architectural consumers, not just applications or users.

In the Autonomous Enterprise, AI agents reason over governed BDC data products rather than loosely curated datasets. That means every architectural decision how data products are modeled, how integrations are designed, how governance is enforced, directly shapes how trustworthy, safe, and scalable AI-driven decisions will be.

Every well-designed data product and reusable integration pattern now serves both human and AI consumption, dramatically reducing the risk of shadow AI built on ungoverned data.

 

How AI-integrated architecture works with BDC

AI-driven execution does not bypass your architectural blueprints. It builds on them.

BDC empowers AI agents across the enterprise by serving as the AI knowledge core. Data products defined in BDC, sourced from SAP applications, BW objects, or Datasphere models. They carry business meaning, lineage, and access rules. BAIP uses these data products as its trusted context layer for AI reasoning and action. When AI agents generate recommendations, trigger workflows, or execute processes, they do so against this governed semantic layer and are designed to inherit the same authorizations and policies you defined.

For enterprise architects, this means designing architectures that expose data products as reusable, governed, AI-consumable assets, not just as feeds into dashboards or reports.

A simple example: a governed revenue KPI data product defined once in SAP Datasphere can be consumed by a planner in SAP Analytics Cloud, a Fiori app, and a BAIP agent that triggers an approval workflow, all using the same semantics and authorizations.

 

Trust, governance, and risk

One of the biggest enterprise AI challenges is trust: How do you ensure AI operates within the same boundaries as human users and applications?

In the Autonomous Enterprise architecture, trust is enforced by design. Because AI agents rely on the same BDC-managed semantics, master data, and access policies used by human consumers. AI outputs are designed to reflect the same KPI logic, hierarchies, filters, and authorization rules. Governance applies consistently whether the consumer is a dashboard, a planner, a business application, or an AI agent.

The result: fewer parallel governance frameworks for AI and compliance, auditability, and risk controls that extend naturally into the AI layer, keeping the architecture defensible under regulatory scrutiny.

 

How does this map to BDC components

For enterprise architects, the strategic component mapping is clear.

  • SAP Datasphere is the semantic backbone of the Autonomous Enterprise, the primary place where governed data products, semantic models, and business context are defined and exposed, providing authoritative definitions for process-critical KPIs and data domains.
  • SAP Analytics Cloud remains the primary consumption layer for analysis, planning, and AI-assisted insight generation.
  • SAP Master Data Governance ensures master data consistency underpins analytical, transactional, and AI-driven outcomes.
  • SAP Reltio delivers an AI-ready, context-rich, trusted data foundation harmonizing across SAP and non-SAP landscapes.
  • SAP HANA Cloud provides a high-performance data foundation that supports advanced analytical queries, vector processing, and low-latency access for applications and AI agents.
  • The embedded Databricks partnership, complemented by Snowflake interoperability, extends BDC with open lakehouse capabilities, enabling AI/ML workloads and unstructured data processing via zero-copy Delta Sharing.
  • The SAP Business AI Platform (BAIP) uses these components as its trusted context layer, enabling AI agents to reason and act on governed enterprise data.

Together, these components form the trusted architectural foundation on which the Autonomous Enterprise runs.

 

What to focus on now

As an enterprise architect, the most valuable preparation is not evaluating new AI tools. It is strengthening the foundation AI will rely on. Concretely, invest in:

  • Well-designed data products with clear ownership, lineage, and reusability.
  • Consistent semantics across SAP and non-SAP domains.
  • Federated governance models that scale across business units.
  • Integration patterns that treat data products as reusable APIs, not point-to-point pipelines.
  • Identity, access, and policy frameworks that extend seamlessly to AI agents.
  • Openness and interoperability leveraging Delta Sharing and open formats to future-proof your architecture.

The stronger your foundation today, the more reliable and trustworthy your AI-driven decisions will be tomorrow.

 

The takeaway

  • The Autonomous Enterprise does not replace enterprise architecture with AI. It makes enterprise architecture the governed foundation on which trustworthy AI depends.
  • The architectures you design, govern, and evolve no longer serve only people and applications. They shape how AI itself reasons and acts across the enterprise.

 

Continue the Conversation

Join the Enterprise Architecture community to share patterns, discuss AI-integrated designs, and see how peers are evolving their BDC architectures.

 

Useful resources:

  • SAP Architecture Center
  • SAP AI-native North Star architecture
  • SAP Business AI Platform
  • SAP Business Data Cloud
  • Blog: A pragmatic roadmap to an autonomous enterprise
  • SAP Learning: Guiding AI-Driven Transformation as an SAP Enterprise Architect
  • SAP Learning: The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous Enterprise

entry.title: What does the Reltio Acquisition Mean for Master Data Management at SAP?
entry.content:
Master data is the foundation of every trusted business process, analytical insight, and AI use case. With the introduction of SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) and the recent acquisition of Reltio, SAP customers now have a more powerful set of choices for how to govern, unify, and trust their master data. Since the SAP Sapphire announcement, one question keeps surfacing from customers and partners alike: When should we choose SAP Master Data Governance (MDG), when should we choose Reltio, and when do we need both?

And if you’re a long-standing SAP Master Data Governance (MDG) customer - like the enterprises that have invested in customizing SAP MDG to fit their global master data management (MDM) and governance processes - SAP’s Reltio acquisition likely triggered a mix of curiosity and concern:

  • Does Reltio replace SAP MDG?
  • What happens to our years of MDG customization and process integration?
  • Where does Reltio fit in with our data, analytics, and AI landscape?
  • How and when do we get started?

The short answer: your SAP MDG investment is preserved, modernized, and extended. Reltio will be a complementary MDM capability inside SAP Business Data Cloud (BDC) with its own set of strengths based on the customer’s desired outcome.

Why Master Data Management (MDM) Matters More Than Ever

SAP’s strategic direction - the Autonomous Enterprise - depends on a strong foundation: the SAP Business AI Platform, which combines deep process context, unified data, and purpose-built models with enterprise governance, so you can build and integrate AI that understands your business, not just your prompts. A core component of that platform is SAP Business Data Cloud, the data foundation that unifies, governs, and delivers AI-ready data across SAP and third-party sources. 

  1. Foundation: Unified Lake Storage
    At the foundation, lake storage unifies SAP and non-SAP data across multi-cloud, hybrid, and on-premises environments. This layer remains essential and enduring.
  2. Intelligent Compute
    Above the foundation, data is processed through intelligent compute, powering data lakehouses, warehouses, machine learning, and data engineering workloads-the areas where most innovation has occurred over the past decade.
  3. The Knowledge Core (What Changes Everything)
    What’s new and truly transformative is above the compute layer. The knowledge core introduces a paradigm where data is no longer merely stored and processed but understood in the context of how the business operates.

This is why metadata is foundational to the entire system. And not just technical metadata like schemas or pipelines, but active, business-aware metadata that carries meaningful context, including definitions of metrics and KPIs, relationships between data entities, business processes and rules, lineage, usage, and quality signals.

This metadata acts as connective tissue, embedding business context directly into the data so it can be consistently understood and acted upon across the entire organization.

  1. Agents and Applications
    This contextual understanding then powers the top layer: agents and applications capable of self-learning and making autonomous decisions.

The true power of this architecture lies in how value compounds with each layer:

  • Intelligent compute delivers speed and scale
  • The knowledge core provides trust and business understanding
  • Agents deliver autonomous action grounded in that understanding

Together, these layers form a continuous cycle rather than a linear pipeline. High quality data informs context, context shapes decisions, and those decisions generate new data that continuously refines and improves the system.

Within this architecture, master data management is being introduced as a part of SAP BDC’s Knowledge Core, delivered through SAP MDG and Reltio as complementary components - both producing unified data products (what MDM experts have historically called the “golden record” or, in Reltio’s terminology, the “trusted record”).

SAP MDG: Central Governance at the Source

SAP Master Data Governance is about getting master data right from the start. It enforces governance within business processes at the point of creation, typically SAP Cloud ERP. Key strengths include:

  • Create trusted, governed master data at the source 
  • Enforce compliance and standards in every process 
  • Distribute directly to applications and to SAP BDC
  • Provide clean master data for all downstream consumption 
  • Ensure full transparency and auditability of changes 

The value promise: Data quality governed at the point of entry.

SAP MDG is now available as a commercial offering inside SAP BDC and delivered as SAP MDG private cloud, extra stack. Bi-directional integration between SAP MDG and the SAP S/4HANA remains a cornerstone of its value.

Reltio: Data Unification Across Any Source

Where SAP MDG governs at the source, Reltio unifies master data across systems including non-SAP applications and unstructured data through cloud-native, real-time integration. Reltio brings two complementary capabilities into the BDC portfolio:

  1. Data Unification
  • Unify master data across all systems into one view 
  • Resolve duplicates and create a consistent, trusted record 
  • Enable 360° visibility across domains (customers, suppliers, products), including unstructured sources 
  • Deliver unified data for any application or data product, with real-time operational access 

The value promise: High-quality trusted records for analytics and AI.

  1. Data Quality & Profiling
  • Assess and monitor data quality in disparate sources 
  • Make data issues visible across domains and systems 
  • Prioritize remediation based on business impact 
  • Continuously improve data quality at scale 

The value promise: Trusted data through ongoing monitoring and remediation.

Reltio is the unique provider enabling the SAP MDM portfolio in BDC to produce unified data products, reflecting a single source of truth.

Better Together: A Complementary Architecture

The vision SAP is executing toward is a single layer of master data management inside SAP BDC, where customers consume MDM as capabilities - spending SAP BDC capacity units - without needing to worry about which underlying component (SAP MDG or Reltio) is doing the work.

Here’s how the complementary architecture works today:

  • Producers (bottom): SAP Cloud ERP, SAP Ariba, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Commerce Cloud, and non-SAP applications feed data into BDC. 
  • Master Data Management in BDC (middle): SAP MDG (central governance at the point of entry in SAP Cloud ERP) and Reltio (cloud-native unification from any source) sit side by side, both producing high-quality master data that contribute towards trusted, unified data products.
  • Consumers (top): Joule agents, SAP CAP applications, SAP Analytics via SAP Datasphere, and SAP & non-SAP business applications consuming data in real time - including AI workloads grounded in trusted data. 

It’s a Choice of Data Strategy

The choice between SAP MDG and Reltio isn’t really an either/or - it’s an architecture driven decision based on an organization’s governance model, source landscape, and desired outcomes:

  • Use SAP MDG when governance and process control at the SAP application source is the priority. 
  • Use Reltio when unification, 360° views, and cross-system data quality - including non-SAP sources - are the priority. 
  • Use both when the desire is governed creation and enterprise-wide unification, which is increasingly the norm for AI-ready enterprises. 

For heavy SAP MDG adopters:

  • Your investment in SAP MDG is preserved and expanded as SAP MDG becomes a SAP BDC component. 
  • Reltio does not replace SAP MDG. Instead, it extends your MDM footprint beyond the SAP application landscape into non-SAP systems, unstructured data, and 360° domain views. 
  • You can begin adopting Reltio for use cases such as cloud-native unification, cross-system data quality, and 360° profile capabilities needed for a modern, AI-ready enterprise.

Final Thoughts

SAP’s long-term vision is clear: one coherent MDM layer inside SAP BDC, delivering high-quality unified data products that fuel analytics, operations, and AI.

For existing SAP MDG customers, the Reltio acquisition isn’t a disruption - it’s an expansion of what SAP MDM can do for you. SAP MDG today is the standard for governing master data at the SAP application source. Reltio brings the cloud-native unification, cross-system data quality, and 360° profile capabilities needed for a modern, AI-ready enterprise.

The most successful MDM customers will treat this not as a product decision, but as an architecture decision - using each solution where it plays to its strengths, and consuming both as capabilities inside SAP Business Data Cloud.
